The temple complex, dedicated to the Hindu god Vishnu, is built of sandstone blocks, with five central towers symbolizing Mount Meru, representing the zenith of Khmer architectural art.

v.t: Cheng Brothers Found Luo School of Neo-Confucianism
v.s: Northern Song Neo-Confucian scholars Cheng Yi and Cheng Hao founded the 'Luo School' in Luoyang, laying the foundation for Song-Ming Neo-Confucianism. They proposed 'li' (principle) as the origin of the universe, advocating 'investigation of things to extend knowledge' and 'preserving heavenly principles and eliminating human desires'. Their ideas were later developed by Zhu Xi and became official state philosophy.

v.t: Emperor Zhezong Comes of Age, Resents Empress Dowager Gao's Domination
v.s: Emperor Zhezong had reached age 14, the traditional age for personal rule, but Empress Dowager Gao continued to rule behind the curtain, refusing to return power. Zhezong was resentful but dared not show it, secretly contacting officials who supported the new laws through eunuchs, preparing for the later 'Shaosheng Restoration'.

v.t: Normans Capture Malta
v.s: Count Roger I of Sicily (a Norman) led his army to capture Malta, ending nearly two centuries of Arab rule on the island. Roger I promised to respect the faith of local Christians, incorporated Malta into the Kingdom of Sicily, and introduced Latin-rite Catholicism.

v.t: Empress Dowager Gao Critically Ill, Still Refuses to Return Power
v.s: Empress Dowager Gao fell gravely ill; Zhezong requested to rule personally, but Gao refused and arranged for conservative ministers to continue their roles. On her deathbed, she advised Zhezong not to listen to the New Policy faction and to 'abide by ancestral laws'. She died the following year.

v.t: Early Advocates of the Crusades Begin Activity
v.s: Peter the Hermit (Pierre l'Ermite) traveled throughout France and Germany preaching about the suffering of pilgrims in Jerusalem, urging Christians to arm themselves for an eastern expedition. His itinerant preaching prepared public opinion for Pope Urban II's call for the First Crusade in 1095.

v.t: Empress Dowager Gao Dies, Emperor Zhezong Takes Personal Rule
v.s: After Empress Dowager Gao's death, Emperor Zhezong began ruling personally. He immediately recalled New Policy faction members such as Zhang Dun and Zeng Bu, preparing to restore Wang Anshi's reforms, and demoted conservative officials like Sima Guang and Lü Gongzhu, a move known as the 'Shaosheng Restoration'. Zhezong's vengeful attacks on conservatives intensified factional strife.

v.t: Malcolm III of Scotland Invades England, Dies in Battle
v.s: King Malcolm III of Scotland led his army south to invade Northumbria in England. He was defeated by English noble Robert de Mowbray at the Battle of Alnwick, and Malcolm was killed. Thereafter, the Norman rulers of England strengthened control over the Scottish border.

v.t: Emperor Zhezong Changes Era to Shaosheng, Launches 'Shaosheng Restoration'
v.s: Emperor Zhezong changed the reign era to 'Shaosheng', meaning 'inheriting the sage's governance'. He ordered the restoration of Wang Anshi's new laws, reinstated New Policy officials such as Zhang Dun and Cai Bian, and exiled conservatives like Lü Dafang, Liu Zhi, Su Shi, and Su Zhe to Lingnan. The Shaosheng Restoration caused further political turmoil in the Northern Song.

v.t: Su Shi Exiled to Huizhou
v.s: Su Shi, listed as a member of the 'Yuanyou Faction', was purged and demoted to be Deputy Military Training Commissioner of Huizhou (present-day Huizhou, Guangdong) with a sinecure. Despite the hardships of life in Lingnan, Su Shi remained optimistic, writing the famous line: 'Eating three hundred lychees a day, I would gladly stay in Lingnan forever.'

v.t: Pope Urban II Preaches the First Crusade at Clermont
v.s: Pope Urban II delivered a speech at the Council of Clermont in France, urging Western Christians to liberate Jerusalem from the Seljuk Turks, promising that participants would receive remission of sins. Tens of thousands of knights and peasants responded, marking the beginning of the First Crusade, which lasted nearly two centuries.

v.t: Shen Kuo Completes Final Revision of Dream Pool Essays
v.s: Shen Kuo completed the final revision of the Dream Pool Essays (Mengxi Bitan) at Mengxi Garden in Runzhou. The book recorded his research in astronomy, geography, physics, chemistry, biology, and medicine, and was the first to document magnetic declination, crustal changes, naming of petroleum, etc. It is a milestone in Chinese scientific history.

v.t: First Crusade Begins
v.s: Tens of thousands of crusaders, led by French and German nobles, set out for Constantinople in four columns. The 'People's Crusade' marched first but was annihilated by the Seljuks in Asia Minor. The noble crusaders captured Nicaea and Antioch in 1097 and took Jerusalem in 1099.

v.t: Song Army Defeats Western Xia at Mount Tiandu
v.s: Song generals Zhe Kequ and Li Xian defeated the army of Western Xia's Empress Dowager Liang at Mount Tiandu (present-day Haiyuan, Ningxia), killing thousands. The Song followed up by capturing Huizhou (present-day Jingyuan, Gansu), and Western Xia sued for peace. This victory was the greatest Song triumph over Western Xia during Emperor Zhezong's reign.

v.t: Crusaders Besiege Antioch
v.s: Led by Bohemond of Taranto, the crusaders laid siege to the city of Antioch (present-day Antakya, Turkey). After eight months of grueling siege, the crusaders captured the city with the help of a traitor, but were soon themselves besieged by a Seljuk relief force. In desperate straits, the crusaders discovered the 'Holy Lance', their morale soared, and they sallied out to rout the Seljuk relief army.

v.t: Zhang Dun Viciously Purges Yuanyou Faction Members
v.s: New Policy leader Zhang Dun, with Emperor Zhezong's support, launched a massive purge of the conservative officials from the Yuanyou era. He ordered the posthumous stripping of ranks and destruction of tombstones of deceased figures like Sima Guang and Lü Gongzhu, and exiled living officials like Su Shi, Su Zhe, and Fan Chunren to the miasmic Lingnan region, with a stipulation that they would never be allowed back into officialdom.

v.t: Song Army Captures Key Western Xia Strongholds
v.s: Song generals Zhe Kequ and Wang Zhan led forces to capture Western Xia's Huizhou, Qingtang Fortress (present-day Minxian, Gansu), and Miaochuan (present-day Ledu, Qinghai), severing the link between Western Xia and Tibet. Emperor Zhezong established the 'Longyou Protectorate General' at Qingtang Fortress, but Western Xia quickly counterattacked, and the Song were unable to hold these fortresses for long.

v.t: Pope Urban II Dies in Rome
v.s: Pope Urban II died in Rome at about age 63. As the initiator of the First Crusade, though he did not live to see Jerusalem captured, his speeches ignited the Crusades that lasted two centuries, profoundly altering the history of Europe and the Middle East.

v.t: Crusaders Capture Jerusalem
v.s: After a five-week siege, the First Crusade broke through the walls of Jerusalem. The crusaders carried out a three-day massacre in the city, reportedly killing tens of thousands of Muslims and Jews. They held a mass at the Church of the Holy Sepulchre, established the Kingdom of Jerusalem, and Godfrey of Bouillon became 'Advocate of the Holy Sepulchre'.

v.t: Emperor Zhezong Dies, Huizong Ascends
v.s: Emperor Zhezong of Song died in Kaifeng at age 24, without sons. Empress Dowager Xiang made Zhezong's younger brother Zhao Ji emperor, known as Emperor Huizong. Huizong loved painting, calligraphy, and Daoism, neglected state affairs, and entrusted governance to treacherous officials such as Cai Jing, setting the Northern Song on the path to destruction.

v.t: Empress Dowager Xiang Acts as Regent, Temporarily Pardons Yuanyou Faction
v.s: In the early days of Emperor Huizong's reign, Empress Dowager Xiang ruled behind the curtain. She issued a decree pardoning the Yuanyou faction and recalling some exiled officials; Han Zhongyan became chancellor. However, Empress Dowager Xiang died the following year, and Huizong immediately reinstated Cai Jing and resumed suppression of the old faction.

v.t: Fatimid Dynasty of Egypt Rebuilds Walls of Jerusalem
v.s: After the crusaders captured Jerusalem, Fatimid Caliph al-Amir rebuilt the damaged walls and strengthened fortifications. The Fatimids and the crusader Kingdom of Jerusalem then entered a long period of stalemate, contending for surrounding towns.

v.t: Great Zimbabwe Culture Enters Flourishing Period
v.s: The Shona people's construction of the Great Zimbabwe stone city on the Zimbabwean plateau reached its peak. The city is built of massive granite blocks without mortar, with walls up to 10 meters high. Great Zimbabwe is the largest ancient stone structure in southern Africa, representing the highly developed political organization and technical ability of the Bantu people.

v.t: European Chivalric System Develops
v.s: At the end of the 11th century, the European knightly class gradually formed a systematic code of conduct known as chivalry (Chivalry). Knights swore to serve their lords, protect the weak, believe in Christianity, and abide by rules of combat and treatment of prisoners. The chivalric system became an important pillar of medieval European society, profoundly influencing literature, warfare, and manners.

v.t: Polynesian Migration to New Zealand
v.s: Polynesian voyagers, using double-hulled canoes and celestial navigation, sailed over 3,000 kilometers from the Society Islands to reach New Zealand. They found the islands uninhabited, with vast forests and moa birds. These migrants became the ancestors of the Maori people, and Maori culture gradually formed in New Zealand.

v.t: Great Zimbabwe Builds Stone City
v.s: Approximately 1100-1450, Great Zimbabwe became the most magnificent stone city in southern Africa. Its great stone buildings ('Great Enclosure' and Acropolis) were dry-built from granite blocks without mortar, with exterior walls up to 11 meters high. Great Zimbabwe was the capital of the Shona kingdom, controlling the gold, ivory, and copper trade between the Indian Ocean and the interior. Excavations have found trade goods from Persia and China.
